topics_flat: Number Theory > Modular Arithmetic > Chinese remainder theorem
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Greatest common divisors (gcd)
Number Theory > Divisibility / Factorization > Prime numbers
prompt: Let $p$, $q$, $r$ be prime numbers such that $p < q < r$ and at least two of $p+n$, $q+n$, $r+n$ are relatively prime for any positive integer $n$. Find all possible values of the pair $(p, q)$.
response_text:
We need determine all prime pairs \((p,q)\) for which there exists some prime \(r>q\) such that

> for every positive integer \(n\), at least two of
> \[
> p+n,\quad q+n,\quad r+n
> \]
> are relatively prime.

We will show this forces \((p,q)=(2,3)\), and that this pair indeed works.

## 1. Reformulate the condition

If for some \(n\), every pair among \(p+n,q+n,r+n\) had gcd \(>1\), then the condition would fail.

So for every \(n\), it is **not** allowed that all three pairwise gcds exceed \(1\).

Now note:
\[
\gcd(p+n,q+n)\mid (q+n)-(p+n)=q-p,
\]
so any common divisor of \(p+n\) and \(q+n\) must divide \(q-p\). Similarly,
\[
\gcd(q+n,r+n)\mid r-q,\qquad \gcd(p+n,r+n)\mid r-p.
\]

This suggests using congruences modulo prime divisors of these differences.

---

## 2. First, \(p\) must be \(2\)

Assume \(p\ge 3\). Then \(p,q,r\) are all odd primes, so for odd \(n\),
\[
p+n,\ q+n,\ r+n
\]
are all even and bigger than \(2\). Hence every pair has gcd at least \(2\). This violates the condition.

Therefore,
\[
p=2.
\]

---

## 3. Then \(q\) must be \(3\)

Assume \(q>3\). Since \(q,r\) are odd primes, both \(q-2\) and \(r-2\) are odd.

Let \(a\) be any prime divisor of \(q-2\), and \(b\) any prime divisor of \(r-2\). Also let \(c\) be any prime divisor of \(r-q\) (note \(r-q\) is even, so \(c\) may be \(2\), but that is fine).

Choose \(n\) by the system
\[
n\equiv -2 \pmod a,\qquad
n\equiv -2 \pmod b,\qquad
n\equiv -q \pmod c.
\]
This is solvable by CRT after combining compatible congruences (the first two are the same residue class mod \(a,b\)); in particular we can choose positive \(n\).

Then:
- \(2+n\equiv 0\pmod a\), so \(\gcd(2+n,q+n)>1\) because both differ by \(q-2\), and in fact \(q+n\equiv q-2\equiv 0\pmod a\).
- Similarly, \(2+n\equiv 0\pmod b\) and \(r+n\equiv r-2\equiv 0\pmod b\), so \(\gcd(2+n,r+n)>1\).
- Also \(q+n\equiv 0\pmod c\) and \(r+n\equiv r-q\equiv 0\pmod c\), so \(\gcd(q+n,r+n)>1\).

Thus all three pairs have gcd \(>1\), contradiction.

Hence \(q\) cannot exceed \(3\). Since \(q\) is prime and \(q>p=2\), we get
\[
q=3.
\]

So the only possible pair is
\[
(p,q)=(2,3).
\]

---

## 4. Check that \((2,3)\) is actually possible

We need some prime \(r>3\) such that for every positive integer \(n\), at least two of
\[
2+n,\ 3+n,\ r+n
\]
are relatively prime.

Take \(r=5\). Then the three numbers are
\[
n+2,\ n+3,\ n+5.
\]

Suppose no two were relatively prime. Then in particular:
- \(\gcd(n+2,n+3)>1\), impossible, since consecutive integers are coprime.

So already the pair \((n+2,n+3)\) is always relatively prime. Hence the condition is satisfied for every \(n\).

Thus \((2,3)\) indeed works.

---

Therefore the only possible pair is:

Final answer: \((2,3)\)
